title: Thailand Street Food — 25 Must-Try Dishes (2026 Guide)
meta_description: The 25 best Thai street food dishes, written by someone who has eaten all of them. Real prices, MICHELIN Bib Gourmand picks, Yaowarat, Chiang Mai Night Bazaar, Chatuchak — a journalist's guide.
intro: Tom yum goong is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Pad Thai was invented as a political act in the 1940s. Mango sticky rice has been served at Kor Panich's Bangkok shophouse since 1932. Thai street food is not backdrop — it is history on a plate. After spending years eating through Bangkok's sois, Chiang Mai's night markets, and the canal-side alley at Victory Monument, this is what actually matters: who made the dish, where it came from, and how to find the version that hasn't been dulled down for tourists. The MICHELIN Guide has been awarding Bib Gourmand distinctions to Thai street food vendors since 2017; the 2026 selection covers 137 venues across the country. Most cost under 100 THB.

items.name: Pad Thai
items.description: Stir-fried sen lek rice noodles with egg, tofu or shrimp, dried shrimp, bean sprouts, and garlic chives, seasoned with tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar, finished with crushed roasted peanuts and a wedge of lime. Despite its global fame, Pad Thai is a relatively modern dish: it was promoted by Prime Minister Plaek Phibunsongkhram's government in the 1940s as a national dish to reduce rice consumption during wartime shortages. Recipes were distributed to every restaurant in the country. Thip Samai on Maha Chai Road, open since 1966, remains Bangkok's most celebrated address — their signature version wraps the noodles in a thin egg net. In Yaowarat (Bangkok's Chinatown), pad thai vendors work over charcoal woks well past midnight.
items.where_to_find: Thip Samai, Maha Chai Road, Bangkok (est. 1966); night markets throughout Yaowarat; Chiang Mai Night Bazaar, Chang Klan Road
key_facts: Promoted as a national dish under PM Phibunsongkhram's 1940s government campaign (Smithsonian Magazine)
Thip Samai, Bangkok — open since 1966, queues form nightly
Yaowarat vendors use charcoal woks for superior wok hei
items.google_maps_query: Thip Samai Pad Thai Bangkok

items.name: Pad Kra Pao
items.description: Minced pork, chicken, or seafood stir-fried with fresh holy basil (kra pao), garlic, bird's eye chilies, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, served over jasmine rice with a crispy fried egg. If pad thai is Thailand's most famous export, pad kra pao is what Thais actually eat for lunch. Walk past any office building at noon in Bangkok and the air smells of holy basil scorching in a wok. Kra pao is not the same as sweet basil (horapha) — it has a clove-like, slightly peppery heat that disappears on cooking, which is why good vendors add it in two waves. Order 'kai dao' for a runny-yolk fried egg on top. The combination of yolk breaking over the spiced rice is the whole point.
items.where_to_find: Every market and street stall in Thailand; Phed Mark by Mark Wiens near BTS Ekkamai; Ung Jia Huad near Asoke (operating 47+ years)
key_facts: Thailand's most consumed daily dish — ordered millions of times daily
Holy basil (kra pao) is distinct from sweet or Thai basil
Order 'kai dao' for fried egg; specify spice level in number of chilies
items.google_maps_query: Phed Mark Pad Krapow Bangkok Ekkamai

items.name: Som Tam
items.description: Shredded green papaya pounded with tomatoes, long beans, dried shrimp, peanuts, garlic, fresh chilies, fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar in a clay mortar. The word 'som tam' combines the Thai words for sour (som) and pounded (tam). Historians trace the dish to Lao communities who were deported to Central Thailand as war captives in the late 18th century, bringing their tradition of pounding fruit salads with them. Papaya had been introduced to the region by Chinese traders. From the Isan (northeastern) heartland, the dish spread nationally with the railway. The MICHELIN Guide's detailed breakdown of som tam variations — published on guide.michelin.com — identifies over ten regional styles. Som tam pla ra (with fermented fish paste) is the Isan original; som tam thai (with peanuts, no fermented fish) is the central Thai adaptation that became the tourist standard. Tell the vendor 'prik song met' (two chilies) if you want to survive.
items.where_to_find: Isan food stalls around Victory Monument, Bangkok; any market in the northeast; Chiang Mai Night Bazaar
key_facts: Originated with Lao settlers in Central Thailand, spreading via the northeastern railway (Wikipedia / MICHELIN Guide research)
MICHELIN Guide identifies 10+ regional variations across Thailand
Tell the vendor your chili count: 'prik song met' (2) to start
items.google_maps_query: Victory Monument Bangkok Som Tam Isan

items.name: Tom Yum Goong
items.description: Hot and sour shrimp soup with lemongrass (ta-khrai), galangal (kha), kaffir lime leaves (bai makrut), fresh chilies, fish sauce, and lime juice — the core aromatic triad that defines Thai cooking is fully expressed here. In December 2024, UNESCO officially inscribed Tom Yum Kung on its Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, making it the fifth Thai tradition to receive the designation alongside Khon masked dance, traditional Thai massage, and Songkran. UNESCO's documentation describes the dish's origin in the Buddhist riverside communities of the Central Plains, where freshwater prawns were the protein of choice and medicinal herbs like galangal and lemongrass were believed to promote wellness. The first written recipe, dated 1888, was for a snakehead fish version; the first mention of shrimp appears in an 1897 missionary food dictionary. The soup comes in two versions: nam sai (clear broth) and nam khon (creamy, enriched with coconut milk and roasted chili paste). T&K Seafood on Yaowarat Road is a long-running address for both.
items.where_to_find: T&K Seafood, Yaowarat Road, Bangkok; Mit Ko Yuan, Dinso Road; any seafood restaurant in Thailand
key_facts: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, inscribed December 2024 (UNESCO, ich.unesco.org)
First written recipe dated 1888; first shrimp version documented 1897
Two versions: clear broth (nam sai) or creamy coconut (nam khon)
items.google_maps_query: T&K Seafood Yaowarat Bangkok

items.name: Mango Sticky Rice
items.thai_name: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง
items.description: Sweet glutinous rice soaked in coconut milk and salt, served alongside slices of ripe Nam Dok Mai mango, topped with a slow-pour of coconut cream and a scattering of mung beans or sesame. The tradition of cooking sticky rice in coconut milk dates to the early Rattanakosin period; over time the pairing with fresh mango became the dominant version. Kor Panich on Tanao Road in Bangkok has been serving the dish since 1932 and is the only mango sticky rice specialist to hold a MICHELIN Bib Gourmand distinction — a recognition that, according to MICHELIN's own listing, elevated it to 'legendary status' among Bangkok food institutions. The Thai variety of mango used is Nam Dok Mai (literally 'water of the flower'), prized for its smooth, non-fibrous flesh and natural sweetness. April to June is peak mango season; out-of-season versions use imported fruit and cost more for less flavour.
items.where_to_find: Kor Panich, Thanon Tanao, Bangkok (est. 1932, MICHELIN Bib Gourmand); Mae Varee, Thong Lo, Bangkok (est. 1981); Chatuchak Weekend Market
key_facts: Kor Panich (est. 1932) holds MICHELIN Bib Gourmand — Bangkok's only dedicated mango sticky rice specialist with the distinction (MICHELIN Guide Thailand 2025)
Best April–June during Nam Dok Mai mango season
Mae Varee on Thong Lo has served since 1981
items.google_maps_query: Kor Panich Mango Sticky Rice Bangkok Tanao

items.name: Khao Soi
items.description: Northern Thailand's most distinctive dish: fresh egg noodles in a rich, fragrant coconut curry broth, topped with crispy deep-fried noodles, pickled mustard greens, shallots, and a squeeze of lime. The broth combines a mild red curry paste with coconut milk and turmeric. Food historians trace khao soi's character to the cultural mixing of Northern Thailand's Lanna Kingdom — the coconut curry base reflects Burmese influence, while the use of egg noodles points to Chinese Muslim (Yunnanese Haw) traders who traveled the overland route from Yunnan province. The dish remains almost entirely Northern Thai; you rarely encounter it south of Lampang. In Chiang Mai, Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kad Kom has carried a MICHELIN Bib Gourmand for several consecutive years. The Night Bazaar on Chang Klan Road in Chiang Mai — which traces its origins to Chinese and local merchants who gathered along that road — is a reliable place to find multiple khao soi vendors in one stretch.
items.where_to_find: Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kad Kom, Chiang Mai (MICHELIN Bib Gourmand); Khao Soi Khun Yai, Chiang Mai; Chiang Mai Night Bazaar, Chang Klan Road
key_facts: Reflects Burmese and Chinese Muslim (Haw trader) cultural influence on Lanna cuisine
Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kad Kom — multiple-year MICHELIN Bib Gourmand recipient (MICHELIN Guide Thailand)
Almost never found south of Lampang — a strictly northern specialty
items.google_maps_query: Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kad Kom Chiang Mai

items.name: Satay
items.description: Thin-cut pork, chicken, or beef marinated in turmeric, coconut milk, coriander, cumin, and lemongrass, threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled slowly over charcoal. Served with a thick peanut dipping sauce and a small bowl of ajat (cucumber, shallot, and chili in sweet vinegar). Satay's origin lies in the port cities of Java and the Malay Peninsula, where Indian kebab technique was merged with Chinese bamboo skewering and regional Southeast Asian spices, including peanuts introduced from the Americas via the Manila galleon trade. It arrived in Thailand from the Malay south, where a strong Muslim population adapted it with pork as the dominant protein — an adaptation that marks Thai satay distinctly from its Malay and Indonesian cousins. On Tha Din Daeng Road near Wongwian Yai, Dee Moo Satay has become a Bangkok institution: their charcoal grill is reportedly long enough to handle thousands of skewers at once, and the stall reportedly moves 7,000 to 8,000 sticks on a busy day.
items.where_to_find: Dee Moo Satay, Tha Din Daeng Road, Bangkok; Chiang Mai Night Bazaar; any night market in Thailand
key_facts: Arrived in Thailand from Malay-Indonesian culinary tradition via Muslim traders (Wikipedia: Satay origins)
Peanut sauce uses peanuts introduced via Manila galleon trade — the sauce dates to 19th century Javanese street vendors
Best grilled slowly over real charcoal — avoid boiled or steamed versions sold in tourist shops
items.google_maps_query: Dee Moo Satay Bangkok Wongwian Yai

items.name: Boat Noodles
items.thai_name: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือ
items.description: Small, intensely flavoured bowls of noodle soup in a dark broth built from soy sauce, five-spice, star anise, and cinnamon — traditionally enriched with pig blood for depth. The tiny serving size is not a modern affectation: boats on Bangkok's canals served shallow bowls to reduce spillage in choppy water, and vendors filled them from a pot balanced over charcoal at the stern. Canal trade peaked in the 1940s and 1950s; by the 1970s Bangkok's khlongs had shrunk and vendors moved to land. Today the legacy survives along Boat Noodle Alley just north of Victory Monument, where a stretch of canal-side restaurants still serve the small bowls — you stack them as you go, which is how the bill is calculated. Order 'nam tok' for the version with pig blood (adds viscosity and richness), 'mai nam tok' without. Guay Jub Ouan Pochana at 408 Yaowarat Road is a separate lineage — a 50-year institution in Chinatown serving guay jub, a peppery rolled rice noodle soup with pork belly, cited by the Tourism Authority of Thailand and holding a MICHELIN Bib Gourmand.
items.where_to_find: Boat Noodle Alley, Victory Monument, Bangkok; Guay Jub Ouan Pochana, 408 Yaowarat Road (MICHELIN Bib Gourmand, 50+ years)
key_facts: Canal origin: vendors served tiny bowls from boats on Bangkok khlongs to prevent spillage (Tourism Authority of Thailand; Khaosod English)
Guay Jub Ouan Pochana, Yaowarat — MICHELIN Bib Gourmand, 50+ years serving (MICHELIN Guide Thailand)
Stack empty bowls to track your order — 10 bowls is a light lunch, 20 is respectable
items.google_maps_query: Boat Noodle Alley Victory Monument Bangkok

items.name: Roti
items.description: A thin, butter-fried flatbread of Indian origin, stretched paper-thin on a hot griddle, folded over fillings of banana, egg, condensed milk, Nutella, or plain, then cut into squares. Thai roti arrived via Indian and Muslim traders — southern Thailand, which has a higher Muslim population and stronger cultural ties to Malaysia, makes the best versions. Roti Mataba on Phra Athit Road in Bangkok's Banglamphu district is the city's most famous address: founded in 1943 by Abdul Kareem, a Muslim trader from southern India who settled in Bangkok, the shop has been in continuous operation for over 80 years. The savory mataba version — filled with spiced minced beef and egg — is closer to the Indian original. Along Khao San Road, late-night roti vendors operate from about 9 PM until 2 AM; the classic banana-and-condensed-milk combination at 30–40 THB is one of the best street desserts in the country.
items.where_to_find: Roti Mataba, Phra Athit Road, Bangkok (est. 1943, Abdul Kareem); Khao San Road carts after 9 PM; Phuket and Krabi night markets
key_facts: Roti Mataba founded 1943 by Abdul Kareem from southern India — 80+ years in Banglamphu (Bangkok Heaven / Travelfish)
Thai Muslim origin via Indian traders; strongest tradition in southern Thailand (higher Muslim population)
Mataba (savory) and sweet versions — banana + condensed milk is the classic dessert form
items.google_maps_query: Roti Mataba Phra Athit Road Bangkok

items.name: Grilled Seafood
items.thai_name: อาหารทะเลย่าง
items.description: Whole fish, tiger prawns, squid, crabs, and shellfish cooked over charcoal at the table or on large street grills — the dominant mode of eating in Thailand's coastal provinces and night markets. In Bangkok, the concentration of seafood grills is highest on Yaowarat Road (Chinatown), where vendors extend onto the pavement from 5 PM as a large food street market is set up along the main road (Tourism Authority of Thailand). T&K Seafood on Yaowarat is a long-running institution for grilled shellfish and crab. In Hua Hin, the Night Market and Khao Takiab seafood market on the northern edge of town serve lobster, shrimp, and whole fish straight off the grill. In Phuket, Rawai Seafood Market allows visitors to buy direct from the catch and have nearby restaurants grill it for a small fee; Banzaan Market in Patong operates a buy-downstairs-cook-upstairs system. The standard accompaniment across all regions is seafood sauce (nam jim seafood) — a sharp, citrusy blend of lime, fish sauce, garlic, and bird's eye chili.
items.where_to_find: Yaowarat Road, Bangkok (5 PM onwards); T&K Seafood, Yaowarat; Hua Hin Night Market; Rawai Seafood Market, Phuket; Banzaan Market, Patong
key_facts: Yaowarat Road sets up a large food street market from 5 PM, closing traffic lanes for vendors (Tourism Authority of Thailand)
Rawai Seafood Market, Phuket: buy-your-catch, nearby restaurants grill it for a small fee
Nam jim seafood (lime, fish sauce, garlic, chili) is the universal dipping sauce
items.google_maps_query: T&K Seafood Yaowarat Bangkok

items.name: Fried Insects
items.description: Deep-fried crickets, grasshoppers, silkworm pupae, bamboo worms, and giant water bugs — known collectively in Thai as ma-laeng tod — seasoned with salt, kaffir lime leaves, and sometimes white pepper. Insect consumption has deep roots in Northern and Northeastern Thailand, where populations have included insects in the regular diet for generations as a sustainable and high-protein food source; it is not a novelty invented for tourism. However, the most visible insect carts for visitors are along Khao San Road in Bangkok, where pushcarts set up from around 6 PM daily. More authentic consumption happens in Isan markets and Chiang Mai's Warorot Market (Kad Luang), where vendors sell insects for home cooking and daily snacking. Crickets and silkworm larvae are available year-round; scorpions, considered a rare delicacy, come from Isan suppliers and are harder to source. The crunch and mild nuttiness of a well-fried cricket is entirely different from the mental image most visitors carry in.
items.where_to_find: Khao San Road carts, Bangkok (from 6 PM); Warorot Market (Kad Luang), Chiang Mai; any Isan market in the northeast
key_facts: Long-standing part of Northern and Northeastern Thai diet — not a tourist gimmick (Nation Thailand; Best Price Travel)
Crickets, grasshoppers, silkworm pupae, and giant water bugs are the most common varieties
Scorpions: rare, sourced from Isan, considered a delicacy by local standards
items.google_maps_query: Khao San Road Bangkok insect cart

items.name: Moo Ping
items.description: Marinated grilled pork skewers — strips of pork shoulder or belly soaked in garlic, coriander root, fish sauce, oyster sauce, coconut milk, and palm sugar, then threaded on bamboo and grilled over charcoal to a deep caramel. Thailand's quintessential breakfast food, sold from 5 AM at morning markets and school gates. The combination of salty-sweet marinade and charcoal smoke is one of the most elemental flavours in Thai street food. Served in sets of 3-4 sticks with glutinous rice in a small plastic bag. Moo Ping Hea Owen in Bangkok's Silom-Sathorn area is frequently cited as the city's busiest moo ping operation, reportedly moving over 2,000 sticks per hour during the morning rush.
items.where_to_find: Morning markets across Thailand from 5 AM; Moo Ping Hea Owen, Silom-Sathorn area, Bangkok; street carts outside school gates and offices
key_facts: Morning market staple — vendors start grilling at 5 AM
Moo Ping Hea Owen, Silom: reportedly 2,000+ sticks per hour at peak
3-4 sticks + sticky rice typically 30-50 THB — the ideal Bangkok breakfast
items.google_maps_query: Moo Ping Hea Owen Silom Bangkok

items.name: Khao Mun Gai
items.thai_name: ข้าวมันไก่
items.description: Thailand's adaptation of Hainanese chicken rice — poached chicken over rice cooked in rendered chicken fat and broth, accompanied by a bowl of clear soup and a sauce made from fermented soybeans, ginger, vinegar, garlic, and chili. The Thai-Chinese community introduced the dish from Hainan province in southern China; it became embedded in Thai food culture through the large Teochew and Hainanese populations in Bangkok and Hat Yai. Go-Ang Pratunam Chicken Rice (known as 'the pink restaurant' for the colour of its staff uniforms) has held a MICHELIN Bib Gourmand for multiple years, serving bowls from its original Pratunam location. At 40-60 THB for a full plate, it is also the most economical way to eat at a MICHELIN-recognised establishment anywhere in the world.
items.where_to_find: Go-Ang Pratunam Chicken Rice, Bangkok (MICHELIN Bib Gourmand, 'the pink restaurant'); Boon Tong Kiat, Soi Polo, Bangkok; Hat Yai markets
key_facts: Go-Ang Pratunam — multiple MICHELIN Bib Gourmand recognitions; known as 'the pink restaurant' (MICHELIN Guide Thailand 2025)
Thai-Chinese origin via Hainanese and Teochew settlers in Bangkok
One of the mildest, most accessible Thai dishes for first-time visitors
items.google_maps_query: Go-Ang Pratunam Chicken Rice Bangkok

items.name: Hoy Tod
items.description: Crispy oyster or mussel omelette — fresh shellfish cooked in a batter of rice flour and tapioca starch with egg and bean sprouts, fried in lard or vegetable oil on a flat iron pan until the edges shatter when prodded. Available in two versions: tod krop (extra crispy, minimal egg binding) and the softer, more custardy standard version. Served with sweet chili sauce. Nai Mong Hoy Tod on the edge of Bangkok's Yaowarat Chinatown has been making hoy tod for over 30 years and is consistently cited as the benchmark, its crispy version having a texture closer to a broken lace curtain than an omelette.
items.where_to_find: Nai Mong Hoy Tod, Yaowarat / Chinatown area, Bangkok (30+ years); Wang Lang Market, Bangkok
key_facts: Ask for 'tod krop' for the extra crispy version — lacy edges, minimal batter
Nai Mong Hoy Tod: 30+ year institution in Yaowarat area
Lard frying is traditional — vegetable oil versions are less crispy
items.google_maps_query: Nai Mong Hoy Tod Yaowarat Bangkok

items.name: Gai Yang
items.description: Whole or half chicken marinated in garlic, coriander root, lemongrass, turmeric, soy sauce, and fish sauce, then grilled slowly over charcoal until the skin turns lacquered and dark gold. Part of the canonical Isan trio alongside som tam and sticky rice. In Khon Kaen, Udon Thani, and Khorat — the three major cities of the northeast — gai yang stalls are open from 7 AM until sold out, usually before noon. The accompanying sauce, nam jim jaew, is made from roasted dried chili, tamarind, fish sauce, shallots, and toasted rice powder. It is one of the most underrated sauces in Thai cooking.
items.where_to_find: Morning Isan markets in Khon Kaen and Udon Thani; SPE Gai Yang, Convent Road, Bangkok; Sabai Jai Gai Yang, Ekkamai
key_facts: Core of the Isan trio: gai yang + som tam + sticky rice
Sold out by noon at most Isan market stalls — arrive early
Nam jim jaew (toasted rice powder dipping sauce) is essential
items.google_maps_query: SPE Gai Yang Convent Road Bangkok

items.name: Guay Teow Noodle Soup
items.thai_name: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว
items.description: The customisable noodle soup that forms the backbone of daily Thai eating. Choose your noodle — sen mee (thin rice), sen lek (medium rice, the most common), sen yai (wide flat rice), or ba mee (egg) — your broth (clear, red tom yum, or dark nam tok), and your protein (pork, chicken, beef, fishballs, or mixed). The condiment caddy that arrives with the bowl is not optional: it contains sugar (sweetness), dried chili flakes (heat), fish sauce (salt), and vinegar with fresh chilies (sour). Thai noodle culture mandates that you adjust the bowl to your own preference. The stall with the glass case displaying its raw ingredients and a long-simmering pot of broth is the standard setup.
items.where_to_find: Every market and soi in Thailand; Sae Phun noodle shop, Bangkok (est. 1926, MICHELIN Bib Gourmand)
key_facts: Sae Phun noodle shop, Bangkok — established 1926, MICHELIN Bib Gourmand; known for chicken stew with deep-fried egg noodles passed down four generations (MICHELIN Guide Thailand 2025)
The 4-jar condiment caddy is meant to be used — no customisation is an incomplete bowl
Sen lek (medium rice noodle) is the default in most shops
items.google_maps_query: Sae Phun noodle shop Bangkok

items.name: Nam Tok Moo
items.description: 'Waterfall pork' — grilled pork sliced thin and tossed while hot with toasted rice powder (khao khua), shallots, fresh mint, cilantro, green onions, dried chili flakes, fish sauce, and lime juice. The name references the juices running off the meat as it grills. The toasted rice powder, made by dry-frying raw glutinous rice then grinding it coarsely, adds a smoky, nutty crunch that is entirely specific to Isan cuisine and appears in larb and other dishes in the same family. Served with sticky rice and raw cabbage to cool the heat. Almost always available at the same Isan stalls that sell som tam.
items.where_to_find: Isan food clusters, Victory Monument, Bangkok; any som tam vendor in the northeast
key_facts: Toasted rice powder (khao khua) is the defining technique — dry-fried glutinous rice, coarsely ground
'Nam tok' refers to the juices dripping from the meat — not a literal waterfall
Raw cabbage is the traditional cooling companion, not a garnish
items.google_maps_query: Isan food stalls Victory Monument Bangkok

items.name: Coconut Ice Cream
items.thai_name: ไอศครีมกะทิ
items.description: Fresh coconut milk ice cream churned in a metal bucket packed with ice and rock salt — no dairy, naturally vegan, with a clean, unsweetened coconut base served in a coconut shell half. Toppings include roasted peanuts, sweet corn, palm seeds, sticky rice, red beans, coco jelly, and mung beans. At Chatuchak Weekend Market — which covers 35 acres with over 15,000 stalls and draws 200,000 visitors weekly (Tourism Authority of Thailand) — Coco JJ in section 1 has been among the market's most popular dessert stops since 2008. The combination of warm, crispy sticky rice topping and cold coconut ice cream is specifically Thai and not replicated anywhere else.
items.where_to_find: Coco JJ, Section 1 Lane 36, Chatuchak Weekend Market, Bangkok; coconut ice cream carts in night markets and floating markets
key_facts: Naturally dairy-free — made entirely from coconut milk, no cream or butter
Coco JJ, Chatuchak Weekend Market: in operation since 2008, section 1 lane 36
Chatuchak: 35 acres, 15,000+ stalls, 200,000+ weekly visitors (Tourism Authority of Thailand)
items.google_maps_query: Coco JJ Chatuchak Weekend Market Bangkok

items.name: Sai Oua
items.description: Northern Thai herbal pork sausage made from minced pork mixed with red curry paste, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, shallots, and dried chilies — aromatic rather than hot, closer to a sausage made from curry paste than anything else in Thai cooking. The difference from Isan sausage (sai krok) is total: where sai krok ferments for sourness, sai oua's character comes entirely from fresh aromatics. A Chiang Mai specialty that rarely travels south. The best versions come from the wet market stalls inside Warorot Market (Kad Luang) in Chiang Mai and the vendors at Chiang Mai Gate night market.
items.where_to_find: Warorot Market (Kad Luang), Chiang Mai; Chiang Mai Gate Night Market; northern Thai food stalls
key_facts: Exclusively Northern Thai (Lanna) — rarely available south of Lampang
Aromatic from fresh herbs, not sour like Isan fermented sausage
Warorot Market (Kad Luang), Chiang Mai — the definitive source
items.google_maps_query: Warorot Market Chiang Mai sai oua

items.name: Sai Krok Isan
items.thai_name: ไส้กรอกอีสาน
items.description: Fermented pork and sticky rice sausage from the northeast — the mixture is packed into casings and left to ferment for three to four days, developing a pronounced sour tang. Grilled over charcoal until the casing is split and charred. Served with raw bird's eye chilies, sliced young ginger, and a wedge of raw cabbage. The sausage itself is mild; the heat comes entirely from the condiments. Standard finger food at morning markets across Isan and at every Isan food cluster in Bangkok.
items.where_to_find: Isan food stalls across Thailand; Victory Monument area, Bangkok; morning markets in Khon Kaen, Udon Thani
key_facts: 3-4 day fermentation creates the sour flavour — intentional, not spoilage
Mild sausage + intensely hot raw chilies served alongside
Morning market staple in Isan; available throughout the day at Bangkok Isan clusters
items.google_maps_query: Sai Krok Isan street food Bangkok market

items.name: Khao Pad
items.description: Thai fried rice: day-old jasmine rice stir-fried over high heat with egg, onion, garlic, and your choice of protein — shrimp, chicken, pork, crab, or mixed seafood — seasoned with fish sauce, light soy sauce, and a squeeze of lime on arrival. The use of day-old rice (drier, lower moisture) is the technical requirement for proper fried rice; fresh rice steams instead of frying. Available at Chatuchak Weekend Market alongside dozens of other dishes, and at every street stall in Thailand. The pineapple fried rice version (khao pad sapparod) — served in a carved pineapple shell — is a Thai-Chinese hybrid that became a Bangkok tourist standard but originated in Chinese-influenced central Thai cooking.
items.where_to_find: Every market and food stall in Thailand; Chatuchak Weekend Market; Yaowarat; Victory Monument area
key_facts: Day-old rice is essential — fresh rice produces steamed rather than fried texture
Ask for 'prik nam pla' (chopped chili in fish sauce) on the side
Pineapple version (khao pad sapparod) is a Thai-Chinese fusion
items.google_maps_query: Chatuchak Market Bangkok fried rice

items.name: Kanom Jeen
items.description: Fresh fermented rice noodles — thin, slightly sour strands produced by fermenting rice for three days before extruding through a sieve into boiling water — served with regional curry toppings. Nam ya (fish curry with galangal and lemongrass) is the central Thai version; nam ngiew (pork, tomato, and blood in a spiced broth) is the Chiang Mai adaptation influenced by Shan and Burmese cuisine; green curry chicken is the Bangkok tourist standard. Morning markets are the primary setting — by 10 AM most vendors have sold out.
items.where_to_find: Morning markets nationwide; Kanom Jeen Buffet Chao Din, MBK Mall 5th floor, Bangkok (all-you-can-eat, 59 THB)
key_facts: Rice noodles fermented 3 days — the slight sourness is intentional and specific to kanom jeen
Nam ngiew (Chiang Mai version) has Shan/Burmese cultural influence
Best before 10 AM at morning markets; often sold out by late morning
items.google_maps_query: Kanom Jeen morning market Bangkok

items.name: Khanom Buang
items.description: Thai crispy crepes — fragile taco-shaped shells made from rice and mung bean flour batter cooked on a convex iron pan, filled with meringue cream and garnished with either sweet toppings (shredded coconut, foi thong egg yolk threads) or savory ones (chopped scallions, dried shrimp). One of the oldest documented Thai street snacks, with recipes appearing in Siamese court literature. The skill is in the batter and the filling technique — the best versions shatter on the first bite.
items.where_to_find: Khanom Buang Phraeng Nara, Phra Nakhon, Bangkok; near temples and historic sites; Damnoen Saduak Floating Market
key_facts: One of the oldest documented Thai street snacks, recorded in historic Siamese court texts
Sweet (egg yolk threads, coconut) and savory (shrimp, scallion) versions equally traditional
The batter must be very thin — thick versions are an inferior shortcut
items.google_maps_query: Khanom Buang Bangkok crispy crepe

items.name: Thai Iced Tea
items.description: Strong brewed Ceylon-style tea mixed with sugar and condensed or evaporated milk, poured over crushed ice. The distinctive deep orange colour was historically achieved with food colouring but premium vendors now use a blend of tea and roasted seed additives including star anise, which contributes both colour and flavour. Also available as cha dum yen (black tea without milk) for those who want the bracing bitter base without the sweetness. The standard format is a clear plastic bag with a straw, not a cup — the bag is the Thai street food vessel of choice because it can be carried in one hand.
items.where_to_find: Every street vendor, market, and food court in Thailand; especially visible at Chatuchak Weekend Market
key_facts: Orange colour: traditional recipe uses food colouring or star anise; premium shops use high-grade tea blends
Cha dum yen = black tea without milk — less sweet, shows the tea quality
Served in a plastic bag with straw — the standard Thai street beverage format
items.google_maps_query: Thai iced tea street vendor Bangkok

items.name: Oliang
items.description: Traditional Thai iced coffee brewed from Robusta beans ground with corn, soybeans, sesame seeds, and cardamom, filtered through a cloth sock (tungdtom) that has been seasoned with years of use, sweetened with sugar and condensed milk, served over cracked ice. The strongest, most characterful coffee drink in Thai street culture — Robusta at the base, aromatics from the grain additions. Morning market coffee carts still use the traditional cloth bag brewing method in Bangkok and across the country. Different from modern 'Thai coffee' cafe drinks, which substitute arabica. At 15-30 THB per glass, it remains one of the country's best-value morning rituals.
items.where_to_find: Morning market coffee carts throughout Thailand; Yaowarat morning coffee vendors; Chatuchak market
key_facts: Brewed through a cloth filter (tungdtom) — the method has been unchanged for generations
Robusta beans plus roasted grains (corn, soy, sesame, cardamom) give the distinctive taste
15-30 THB per glass at street carts — one of Thailand's best-value beverages
items.google_maps_query: Oliang Thai coffee street vendor Bangkok morning market

tips: Bangkok's Yaowarat Road (Chinatown) is the city's premier street food zone. After 5 PM, vendors close lanes of traffic and set up along the main road and side alleys. Come hungry: seafood grills, guay jub noodles, oyster omelettes, pad thai, and dim sum all within a 500-metre stretch. The Tourism Authority of Thailand lists Guay Jub Ouan Pochana at No. 408 as a 50-year institution on the road. (Source: tourismthailand.org)

Chiang Mai Night Bazaar on Chang Klan Road opens around 5 PM and runs to 11 PM. The market began as a gathering of Chinese merchants and local traders along the road. Today it is one of Northern Thailand's largest night markets. Street food here is Northern Thai-focused: khao soi, sai oua, nam ngiew, and satay are all easy to find in one walk. (Source: chiangmaihub.com)

Chatuchak Weekend Market covers 35 acres, holds over 15,000 stalls, and attracts more than 200,000 visitors each weekend (Tourism Authority of Thailand). The market operates Saturday and Sunday, 9 AM to 6 PM. Take the MRT to Chatuchak Park station (Exit 1) or Kamphaeng Phet (Exit 2). For street food, focus on the interior food lanes and the perimeter stalls. Arrive by 9 AM to avoid heat and crowds.

MICHELIN Bib Gourmand in Thailand covers 137 venues as of the 2026 selection, spanning Bangkok, Chiang Mai, Phuket, and several other provinces. These are not fine dining awards — they recognise extraordinary food value, and many holders are simple shophouses or stalls. Kor Panich (mango sticky rice, est. 1932), Go-Ang Pratunam (chicken rice), Sae Phun (noodles, est. 1926), and Guay Jub Ouan Pochana (Yaowarat) are all on the current list. (Source: guide.michelin.com/en/th)

Tom Yum Goong was inscribed as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage in December 2024 — Thailand's fifth tradition to receive this recognition, joining Khon masked dance, traditional Thai massage, and Songkran. It is the first Thai dish to be recognised. (Source: ich.unesco.org/en/RL/tomyum-kung-01879)

Look for vendors with high turnover and visible Thai customers eating. High turnover means fresher ingredients. The 'queue of locals' signal is reliable. Tourist-facing stalls with laminated English menus are often more expensive and calibrated for foreign flavour preferences, not Thai ones.

The 4-jar condiment set at noodle stalls is not decoration: sugar (sweetness), dried chili flakes (heat), fish sauce (saltiness), and vinegar with fresh chilies (sourness). Thais use all four to adjust every bowl. Start with small additions — fish sauce in particular is saltier than it smells.

Sticky rice (khao niao) is eaten with your hands in Isan and Northern cuisine — pinch a small ball, press it flat, use it to scoop meat or dip it into sauce. Do not use a fork for sticky rice at a market stall. This marks you as a tourist; it also produces a worse eating experience.

Som tam vendors will ask how many chilies you want. 'Prik song met' means two chilies, which is a mild Thai standard. If you want no chili at all, say 'mai sai prik'. Do not let them add their default amount without specifying — the default for Isan vendors is designed for Isan palates, which are calibrated for significant heat.
